title: The 21st Century Sci-Fi Canon, ‘The Creator,’ and ‘No One Will Save You’
format: Sean and Chris Ryan review Brian Duffield's 'No One Will Save You' and Gareth Edwards' 'The Creator,' then spend the back half building a ten-movie canon of 21st century sci-fi.

films.title: No One Will Save You
films.note: Nearly wordless alien-invasion chase movie starring Kaitlyn Dever. Sean first watched it on an edible while quarantining with COVID, then again sober in a Tulsa hotel room, and liked it both times.

films.title: Spontaneous
films.note: Duffield's directorial debut, about a wave of spontaneous combustions in a high school, released during COVID.

films.title: Prey
films.note: The Predator spinoff Sean compares it to for tone and near-silent execution. A sequel has since been greenlit.

films.title: Monsters
films.note: Gareth Edwards' first film gets pulled in as the other do-a-lot-with-a-little touchstone.

films.title: Invasion of the Body Snatchers
films.note: Sean's shorthand for the movie's genre DNA: 'some combination of Invasion of the Body Snatchers meets Buster Keaton.'

films.title: The Creator
films.note: John David Washington hunts an AI-weapon inventor in a future US-vs-New Asia war. $86 million budget, shot largely in Vietnam, Cambodia, and Nepal. Both hosts call it gorgeous to look at and dramatically thin.

films.title: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
films.note: Edwards' prior movie, and the one Chris thinks The Creator is quietly trying to redo without Tony Gilroy's rewrite.

films.title: Godzilla
films.note: Edwards' American debut. Sean: amazing in the first 20 minutes, then dull except when it's amazing, the same problem he sees in The Creator.

films.title: Demon Seed
films.note: Donald Cammell's Julie Christie-starring AI-home-invasion movie, Sean's go-to 70s comparison for a studio letting an artist make something genuinely strange.

films.title: Ex Machina
films.note: Chris's pick for the movie that renders The Creator moot: same AI-consciousness questions, tighter and more successful.

films.title: Annihilation
films.note: Comes up as another Garland movie willing to leave questions unanswered, which The Creator isn't.

films.title: Blade Runner
films.note: The template for AI-that-acts-human The Creator's robot characters are riffing on.

segments.heading: The 21st Century Sci-Fi Canon
segments.note: the ten they landed on, in the order they came up

films.title: Interstellar
films.note: Chris's line in the sand: Nolan's most visually powerful film, and to Sean also his most gloppy, sentimental, over-explained.

films.title: Ex Machina
films.note: Locked in as one of the ten, on top of its earlier comparison duty against The Creator.

films.title: Looper
films.note: Rian Johnson's time-hopping assassin story, one of the last great Bruce Willis performances.

films.title: Prometheus
films.note: Ridley Scott's messier, more ambiguous Alien prequel, chosen over its own sister film The Martian for being the one they think about more.

films.title: Under the Skin
films.note: Scarlett Johansson as a predatory alien; called out for a visual language nobody's matched in 40 years.

films.title: Her
films.note: Spike Jonze's story of a man falling for an AI voiced by Scarlett Johansson, praised for building futurism out of soft, ordinary clothes instead of chrome.

films.title: Sunshine
films.note: Danny Boyle's sun-mission movie. A 2001-style first two-thirds that both hosts admit collapses into a monster movie by the end.

films.title: Arrival
films.note: Adapted from Ted Chiang; Sean's pick for the film that captured the human heart of this stuff best of the batch.

films.title: Edge of Tomorrow
films.note: The grounded-blockbuster slot, picked over Minority Report as the one they'd rather rewatch.

films.title: Attack the Block
films.note: Joe Cornish's council-estate alien invasion, added as the pure-fun B-movie pick to round out the ten.

films.title: Minority Report
films.note: The blockbuster runner-up to Edge of Tomorrow. Chris calls it the conclusion of the Philip K. Dick era, set in a future that still looks foreign rather than around the corner.

films.title: The Martian
films.note: Prometheus's sunnier sibling, much more winning and feel-good, but the one they think about less.

films.title: Guardians of the Galaxy
films.note: The most recognizable non-Star Wars sci-fi of the last 20 years, per Chris, but not enough to crack the list.

films.title: Snowpiercer
films.note: Bong Joon Ho's class-warfare train movie. Chris predicts his then-upcoming Mickey 17 will resemble it more than anything else he's made.

films.title: Melancholia
films.note: Lars von Trier's end-of-the-world movie, paired with Snowpiercer as two sides of the same fatalist coin.

films.title: Avatar
films.note: Included almost begrudgingly as the highest-grossing franchise ever, despite neither host being a big Na'vi guy.

films.title: Coherence
films.note: A dinner-party-gone-wrong Twilight Zone riff from writer-director James Ward Byrkit, who hadn't made another film since.

films.title: Donnie Darko
films.note: Chris admits he's not a Richard Kelly guy and barely remembers the plot.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: The Daniels' Best Picture winner. Sean's watching with interest to see if its influence actually shows up anywhere.

films.title: After Yang
films.note: The low-budget, indie-scale sci-fi Chris hopes No One Will Save You's success clears more room for.

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: Yorgos Lanthimos's next one, out later that year. The hosts pitch him as the director who should really take a swing at big sci-fi.

films.title: The Lobster
films.note: Chris's case that Lanthimos has basically already done sci-fi once, in absurdist form.

films.title: Twelve Monkeys
films.note: Terry Gilliam comparison for what a Lanthimos-does-sci-fi movie might look like.

films: Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny

Rogue One: A Star Wars Story.title: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story

Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny.title: Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny
